Engineering Work

Projects

Jan 2025 – Dec 2025

Low-Cost X-Ray Inspection Robot

▸ Capstone · Bethlehem Police Department (Bomb Squad)

Developed a ~$1,500 portable X-ray inspection robot for the Bethlehem Bomb Squad — a fraction of the $100,000+ cost of commercial alternatives. Led CAD design of the motorized gear-rail X-ray panel deployment subsystem. Performed cable tension, actuator force, and geometry analyses, and applied FMEA and Pugh matrices to guide risk-based design decisions across a team of four.

SolidWorks CADFMEAPugh MatrixActuator AnalysisPrototype FabricationTeam of 4

With Lt. Fuller, Bethlehem Police Bomb Squad · Lehigh Capstone Expo, May 2025

Jan 2026 – Apr 2026

Auxetic Structure FEA & Material Testing

▸ Lehigh University

Built ABAQUS finite element models of auxetic AA6061-T6 structures with a 7-case mesh convergence study. Computed an effective Poisson's ratio of −0.70 (<2% discretization error). The U1 lateral displacement contour directly characterizes the auxetic effect — the structure expands sideways when pulled vertically. Validated against destructive Instron testing with fracture loads matching within 3.5%.

ABAQUS FEAAA6061-T6Instron TestingMesh ConvergencePoisson's Ratio −0.70

Machined AA6061-T6 specimen

U1 lateral displacement — auxetic expansion (ν = −0.70)

Mar 2026 – May 2026

Engine Performance & Vehicle Dynamics Analysis

▸ Lehigh University

Built a full vehicle performance model from dynamometer data on a turbocharged 6.2L V8 engine, incorporating drivetrain, traction limits, aerodynamic drag, and rolling resistance for a Toyota Camry platform. Applied First-Law thermodynamic energy-balance to quantify system losses, engine efficiency, and fuel economy.

MATLABPowertrain SimulationThermodynamicsDynamometer DataVehicle Dynamics

Turbocharged 6.2L V8 on the dynamometer

Feb 2025 – May 2025

Injection-Molded Miniature Car — 2nd Place

▸ Design & Manufacturing Project · Lehigh University

Partnered with a student from Broughal Middle School whose concept was a Nintendo Switch controller-shaped miniature car. Translated the customer's vision into a manufacturable design using SolidWorks, balancing aesthetic fidelity with injection molding constraints. Programmed CNC toolpaths in Mastercam to mill the aluminum molds, produced the parts on an injection molding machine, and delivered them for the customer to finish with a custom paint job. Scored 2nd place on class-wide race day.

SolidWorksMastercamCNC MillingInjection MoldingDFMCustomer Requirements2nd Place

SolidWorks CAD — vehicle design

CNC-milled aluminum injection mold

Finished product — injection molded

2nd place — class race day
